findings.title: Content-Security-Policy Header Omitted
findings.observation: No Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header was observed on the response.
findings.aiAnalysis: No CSP header was observed on the scanned response. However, no active XSS exploit vector was successfully demonstrated. The finding is treated as a best-practice hardening recommendation rather than evidence of exploitable risk.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: A missing CSP header is a common observation. Modern client-side frameworks and CDNs may mitigate this. In the absence of demonstrated XSS, this remains a hardening advisory. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: Lack of CSP increases the impact of future script injection (XSS) bugs, allowing session hijacking.
findings.remediation: Implement a strict Content-Security-Policy header, setting default-src to self and whitelisting trusted hosts.

findings.description: No Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header was observed on the response.
findings.reasoning: No CSP header was observed on the scanned response. However, no active XSS exploit vector was successfully demonstrated. The finding is treated as a best-practice hardening recommendation rather than evidence of exploitable risk.

findings.title: Strict-Transport-Security Preloaded (Mitigated)
findings.observation: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header is missing on the raw response, but the domain is preloaded in browsers.
findings.evidence: Domain is in HSTS Preload registry
findings.detectionLogic: Match hostname against HSTS Preload registry
findings.aiAnalysis: No HSTS header was observed. However, the target is a known enterprise domain preloaded in modern browsers via HSTS Preload lists. Therefore, the lack of an explicit HSTS header on the response does not present an exploitable risk.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: HSTS preload lists are built directly into modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari), which force HTTPS immediately before any connection. This fully mitigates the risk. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: None. Browser security enforcement prevents any plain-text transmission to this domain.
findings.remediation: No remediation required. The domain is fully secured via the HSTS Preload registry.

findings.description: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header is missing on the raw response, but the domain is preloaded in browsers.
findings.reasoning: No HSTS header was observed. However, the target is a known enterprise domain preloaded in modern browsers via HSTS Preload lists. Therefore, the lack of an explicit HSTS header on the response does not present an exploitable risk.

findings.title: Missing X-Content-Type-Options Header
findings.observation: The X-Content-Type-Options header is missing, allowing browsers to MIME-sniff response content types.
findings.aiAnalysis: No X-Content-Type-Options header was observed. Browsers may attempt to parse files (like images or user uploads) as JavaScript, presenting an XSS vector if file upload controls are weak.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: Without a file upload vector or injection sink, MIME sniffing does not present a direct exploit path. Downgraded to Best Practice. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: Low. Only creates risk if users can upload arbitrary files that the browser might parse as script.
findings.remediation: Configure your web server to return X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff for all HTTP responses.

findings.description: The cookie "__Secure-STRP" (Session Identifier) is not protected with the HttpOnly attribute, allowing client-side scripts to access it.
findings.reasoning: The session cookie "__Secure-STRP" lacks HttpOnly. This is reported as a probable misconfiguration because while the flag is missing, session theft requires a secondary script injection (XSS) vulnerability to exploit.

findings.title: Cookie Missing Secure Flag: NID
findings.observation: The cookie "NID" (Preference/Analytics) is missing the Secure flag, allowing transmission over insecure channels.
findings.evidence: Set-Cookie: NID=532=kSIeLK7IWyzliWNA9HKOSYu3ffSyoKWqckHNH1Wptxn0twNDhUY3vtuuhWvnN7Cg8XyrOquUGkH9KuzlN0OPHyVp9Tifxk82a_N_stz63qcR8YghVGRDGo_2a5AhrWKhxR-ZQfvjnrT1rTf1kV5WenuLYAHaKG_a8kTNchuFolM09yja01g5Rvhsu00DvswwfVZCsyB6iUW6NpThF-GL3bU; expires=Thu, 10-Dec-2026 09:43:08 GMT; path=/; domain=.google.com; HttpOnly
findings.detectionLogic: Match secure parameter in set-cookie headers
findings.aiAnalysis: The analytics/preference cookie lacks the Secure flag. This is a low-risk hardening recommendation.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: The cookie does not handle session metadata, making the lack of Secure flag low risk. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.remediation: Add the 'Secure' flag to ensure the cookie is only transmitted over HTTPS.

findings.description: The cookie "NID" (Preference/Analytics) is missing the SameSite flag, rendering users vulnerable to CSRF.
findings.reasoning: The cookie lacks SameSite. This is a low-risk hardening recommendation.

allFindings.title: Content-Security-Policy Header Omitted
allFindings.observation: No Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header was observed on the response.
allFindings.aiAnalysis: No CSP header was observed on the scanned response. However, no active XSS exploit vector was successfully demonstrated. The finding is treated as a best-practice hardening recommendation rather than evidence of exploitable risk.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: A missing CSP header is a common observation. Modern client-side frameworks and CDNs may mitigate this. In the absence of demonstrated XSS, this remains a hardening advisory. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.businessImpact: Lack of CSP increases the impact of future script injection (XSS) bugs, allowing session hijacking.
allFindings.remediation: Implement a strict Content-Security-Policy header, setting default-src to self and whitelisting trusted hosts.

allFindings.description: The Referrer-Policy header is missing, which could leak sensitive parameters or tokens in referrer headers to external hosts.
allFindings.reasoning: No Referrer-Policy header was observed. The browser's default behavior applies, which might send URL paths including query strings to third parties.

allFindings.title: Missing Permissions-Policy Header
allFindings.observation: The Permissions-Policy header is missing, leaving browser API feature permissions unrestricted.
allFindings.aiAnalysis: No Permissions-Policy header was observed. Interactive browser APIs (camera, geolocation, microphone) can be accessed by the site or framed third-party scripts.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: If the application does not load third-party scripts or handle sensitive device sensors, the practical risk is minimal. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.businessImpact: Enables loaded scripts to request access to browser device capabilities without restrictions.
allFindings.remediation: Implement a Permissions-Policy header restricting access to browser capabilities not utilized by the application.
